entries.title: Collaborator overview - what OAST is and why it matters for blind vulnerabilities
entries.body:
# Burp Collaborator - overview

**Collaborator** is an externally hosted "trap" server that Burp uses to catch vulnerabilities that leave no trace in the application's response. The idea is simple: you slip a special address belonging to the Collaborator server into a request, then watch whether the vulnerable application reaches back out to that address on its own. If it does, the payload worked, even if you saw absolutely nothing in the HTTP response.

This approach is called **OAST** (Out-of-band Application Security Testing) - testing over an "external" channel. Its opposite is the usual in-band test, where the result of an injection is visible right in the response body (reflected XSS, SQL error text, a changed page size). OAST is needed exactly when the in-band channel stays silent.

## Why OAST is needed: the problem of blind vulnerabilities

A huge class of bugs is *blind*: the vulnerability exists, but the application does not return the result of triggering it to you. Without an external channel such bugs are nearly impossible to either detect or prove. Collaborator closes that gap and makes the following observable:

- **Blind SSRF** - the server, at your command, makes a request to a third-party address itself. You put the Collaborator URL into a parameter (image fetch by link, webhook, import by URL, PDF render) and wait for an incoming HTTP/DNS. It arrived - SSRF confirmed.
- **Blind XXE** - the XML parser processes an external entity. An external DTD or an entity with a Collaborator address forces the parser to reach outward; an incoming interaction = the parser really does resolve external entities.
- **Blind OS command injection / RCE** - the injected command does not print its result in the response, but it can make a network call: `nslookup <payload>`, `curl <payload>`, `ping <payload>`. A DNS query that arrives at Collaborator proves command execution.
- **Blind SQL injection** - the DBMS is able to initiate external connections (for example, via DNS resolution in specific functions). An injection that forces the database to resolve a Collaborator subdomain reveals blind SQLi and even lets you exfiltrate data through subdomains.
- **Blind / asynchronous XSS** - the payload fires not at you, but later and somewhere else (in the admin panel, in logs, in a mail client). A script that loads a resource from Collaborator, when triggered, leaves a mark in the interaction log - even if you never see the actual execution.

The common denominator: in all these cases the only reliable signal is a **network interaction that the vulnerable system initiates outward**. Collaborator is exactly what catches such interactions.

## How it works: one server, several protocols

Collaborator is a publicly accessible server that accepts connections over several protocols at once and records each one:

- **DNS.** The server serves its domains in full: for *any* name query in its zone (including arbitrary subdomains) it answers with its own IP address. This is the key point - a DNS interaction is recorded even if it never got as far as an HTTP request. Frequently an application resolves a name first (DNS) and only then connects (HTTP/SMTP), so a DNS ping is the most sensitive indicator.
- **HTTP and HTTPS.** A web service is running with a valid, CA-signed wildcard TLS certificate for its domains. Thanks to this, even strict clients that validate the certificate happily establish an HTTPS connection - the interaction will not fall apart because of a TLS error.
- **SMTP and SMTPS.** A mail service catches incoming email. Useful when the application sends mail to an address you control (injection into an email field, mail headers, etc.).

## Payloads, subdomains, and polling

Each Collaborator payload is a **unique subdomain** of its domain. Burp generates a random label (a long pseudo-random string), and by which subdomain "lit up" in an incoming interaction, Burp understands *which of your payloads* fired. This is the correlation: each tab/each tool sees only interactions from its own payloads, and other people's do not mix in with yours.

Burp itself works on a **polling** model: it periodically (by default about once every ~60 seconds) polls the Collaborator server - "has anything arrived at my subdomains?" - and pulls in new interactions. No inbound channel to your machine is needed: the Collaborator server is what sticks out to the outside, and your Burp merely goes to it to fetch the results.

## Public server and oastify.com

By default Burp uses the **public Collaborator server** that PortSwigger maintains. Historically its subdomains lived on `burpcollaborator.net`, and later the `oastify.com` domain was added - which is why modern payloads look like `<random-string>.oastify.com`. Several domains are needed to reduce the chance that an address ends up on WAF blocklists and the interaction gets blocked (which would give a false negative). If using the public server is not possible for some reason (client policy, isolation requirement), Collaborator can be deployed as **private** - there is a separate section about settings for that.

## Edition

The full Collaborator functionality (payload generation, the interaction log, integration with **Scanner** for automatic discovery of blind bugs) belongs to **Burp Suite Professional**. It is precisely thanks to Collaborator that the scanner is able to find a whole layer of out-of-band vulnerabilities at all that would otherwise stay invisible.

## The essentials in two lines

- A blind vulnerability is not visible in the response - it is visible from the fact that the vulnerable system reaches outward on its own.
- Collaborator gives you that "outward": a unique address for the payload and a log of all incoming DNS/HTTP/SMTP interactions for it.

entries.title: Getting started - generating a payload, inserting it into a request, reading interactions
entries.body:
# Getting started with Collaborator

Working with **Collaborator** comes down to three steps: get a payload, insert it into a request to the target, then wait for and read the incoming interactions. Let us go through each step with hands-on practice.

## Step 1. Generate a payload

Open the **Collaborator** tab. This is where you generate the addresses you will put into the target.

- In the **Payloads to generate** field, specify how many payloads you need (you can generate a whole batch at once - handy when you place markers in many spots of a request or hand out one per parameter).
- The **Include Collaborator server location** checkbox controls the form of the payload:
  - **enabled** - the payload will include the full server address, i.e. a ready FQDN of the form `<random-string>.oastify.com`. This is what you need in most cases: the resulting string can be inserted right away as a host/URL.
  - **disabled** - you will get only the identifier (the random label itself) without the domain part. This is needed in special scenarios where you append the domain part yourself (for example, the payload must go into the middle of an already existing name or is assembled in pieces).
- Click **Copy to clipboard** - Burp will put the generated payloads on the clipboard, from which you paste them anywhere.

Example of a copied payload (full address):

```text
204119i326shak9tnk6k36z8jlahj74r.oastify.com
```

Note: the specific payload domain may change over time - PortSwigger periodically adds new infrastructure domains, so do not "hardcode" the assumption that it is always `oastify.com`.

## Step 2. Insert the payload into a request

There are two working paths.

**Path A - manually via the clipboard.** You generated a payload, copied it with **Copy to clipboard**, switched to **Repeater** (or **Intruder**), and pasted the address into the right place in the request: into a parameter's URL, into a header, into the body, into an XML entity - wherever you suspect an out-of-band trigger.

Examples of substitution:

Blind SSRF - a parameter through which the server fetches a resource itself:

```http
POST /api/fetch-avatar HTTP/2
Host: app.example.com
Content-Type: application/json

{"imageUrl":"https://204119i326shak9tnk6k36z8jlahj74r.oastify.com/x.png"}
```

Blind OS command injection - we inject a network call inside the command:

```http
GET /ping?host=127.0.0.1%3Bnslookup+204119i326shak9tnk6k36z8jlahj74r.oastify.com HTTP/2
Host: app.example.com
```

Blind XXE - an external entity with a Collaborator address:

```xml
<?xml version="1.0"?>
<!DOCTYPE r [ <!ENTITY x SYSTEM "http://204119i326shak9tnk6k36z8jlahj74r.oastify.com/"> ]>
<r>&x;</r>
```

**Path B - via the context menu (the fastest).** In the request editor (for example, in **Repeater**), select the chunk you want to replace with a Collaborator address - say, the URL value in a header - right-click and choose **Insert Collaborator payload**. Burp will generate a fresh payload itself and substitute it for the selected text. This saves manual copying and guarantees that a new unique address is used each time.

After the substitution, send the request as usual (**Send**).

## Step 3. Poll and read interactions

Now you need to check whether the application reached your address.

- Burp polls the server automatically - by default about **once every 60 seconds**, so by the time you return to the **Collaborator** tab, some interactions may already be in the list.
- Do not want to wait for the poll cycle - click **Poll now**, and Burp will immediately go fetch fresh interactions.

In the interactions table each row is a single recorded hit on your payload. Pay attention to the **type**:

- **DNS** - the application (or a system behind it) resolved your subdomain. This is the earliest and most sensitive signal: a DNS query often arrives even when it never got as far as HTTP (for example, outbound HTTP is blocked by a firewall, but DNS resolution is not). A single DNS hit is already weighty proof that the payload fired.
- **HTTP** (or HTTPS) - the application actually established a web connection with the Collaborator server. This is a "stronger" confirmation: it means the target did not just resolve the name but went after the content. Clicking the row shows you the details of the incoming request - method, path, headers (including `User-Agent`, which sometimes reveals which exact infrastructure component made the request), source IP.
- **SMTP** - mail arrived; characteristic of injections into mail fields.

Important about correlation: the tab shows interactions **only from payloads it generated itself**. So if you see a hit, it is definitely yours, and the specific subdomain tells you exactly which of the markers you placed fired. This lets you place different payloads in different parameters and, from the incoming interaction, say unambiguously which field is vulnerable.

## A typical working cycle

```
1. Collaborator: set Payloads to generate, if needed clear/set
   Include Collaborator server location -> Copy to clipboard
   (or right in Repeater: right-click -> Insert Collaborator payload)
2. Insert the address into a suspicious place in the request -> Send
3. Collaborator tab -> Poll now
4. Is there a DNS/HTTP row with your subdomain? -> vulnerability confirmed
5. Click the row -> dissect the details (type, IP, headers) for the PoC and report
```

## Tips from practice

- **Look at DNS first.** Absence of HTTP while DNS is present is not a failure but information: outbound web may be closed, but the vulnerability itself exists.
- **Different payloads for different parameters.** Place a unique address in each suspicious field - then an incoming hit immediately shows the guilty parameter, without guessing.
- **Hit Poll now rather than waiting a minute.** During active manual testing the 60-second cycle is annoying - manual polling gives a result right away.
- **Save the interaction details for the report.** An incoming request with your unique subdomain and a timestamp is exactly the rock-solid PoC of a blind vulnerability: it shows that the target's server, in response to your input, reached out to an address you control.

entries.title: Collaborator settings - public and private server, polling
entries.body:
# Collaborator settings

The behavior of **Collaborator** is configured in the project settings (the Collaborator section). Here you choose which server to use - public or private - and how Burp will poll it for interactions. These settings can be kept at the user level (for all projects) or overridden for the current project only via the **Override options for this project only** toggle.

## Choosing a server

Three options are available:

- **Use the default Collaborator server** - the public PortSwigger server (domains `oastify.com`, historically also `burpcollaborator.net`). The default and simplest option: nothing to deploy, everything works out of the box. Suitable for most bug bounty tasks and ordinary pentesting.
- **Use a private Collaborator server** - your own Collaborator instance (see below).
- **Don't use Burp Collaborator** - fully disable the functionality. Use this when the rules of engagement with the client explicitly forbid any out-of-band requests to external servers.

## Public versus private: which to choose

This is first and foremost a question of trust and control over data.

The **public server** is convenient and requires no infrastructure, but when a payload fires, part of the target's data (for example, content exfiltrated through DNS subdomains or the headers of the incoming HTTP request) ends up on a server that PortSwigger maintains. For many programs this is acceptable, but not for all.

A **private server** is your own Collaborator deployed on infrastructure you control. Reasons to switch to it:

- The client/program does not permit "carrying out" interactions to a third-party public service.
- You need to work in an isolated or internal segment where the public `oastify.com` is unreachable, but your internal host is.
- You want your own domain (less "burned", rarer on WAF blocklists than the well-known public one).
- You require full control over the interaction log and its storage.

The price of the private option is that you need to stand up and maintain a server: a dedicated domain, DNS delegated to it (NS records), and, for full HTTPS, a valid wildcard TLS certificate for that domain.

## Private server parameters

When you select **Use a private Collaborator server**, you set:

- **Server location** - the domain (or IP) of your Collaborator instance. There is an important caveat here: for DNS interactions to work you need a **domain**, not a bare IP - NS records pointing to your server must be delegated to the domain name. With an IP alone the DNS functions will not work.
- **Polling location** (optional) - a separate address at which Burp fetches (polls) interactions, if it differs from the main one. You can specify a hostname or IP and, if needed, a non-standard port in the form `10.20.30.40:8008`. This is handy when the polling service is hosted at a different address/port than the one the payloads themselves arrive at.

## Polling parameters

- **Poll over unencrypted HTTP** - allow polling over plain HTTP instead of the default HTTPS with TLS validation. By default Burp polls the server over a secured channel; this checkbox is needed if direct HTTPS polling runs into network restrictions (for example, in an internal environment where TLS to the polling server is unavailable). Enable it deliberately - it lowers the security of the polling channel.
- **Run health check** - a health check: Burp confirms that the configured server is reachable, returns interactions correctly, and is configured properly overall. Run it right after setting up a private server - it is a quick way to make sure DNS is delegated, ports are open, and polling goes through, before you start the actual test.

A reminder about the automatic polling interval: by default Burp pulls in new interactions about once every 60 seconds, and you can speed this up manually with the **Poll now** button on the **Collaborator** tab (see the getting started section).

## Practical notes

- **The public server is enough by default.** Do not overcomplicate: a private Collaborator is needed for isolation, data control, or working on an internal network - without these reasons the public server is simpler and more reliable.
- **A ban is also a setting.** If the program rules forbid out-of-band traffic, explicitly select **Don't use Burp Collaborator** so you do not accidentally send an interaction outward.
- **Stood up a private one - run Run health check right away.** The lion's share of private Collaborator problems are undelegated DNS or a closed port; the health check catches them before you start work.
- **Override for the project.** If one client needs a private server and another needs a public one, keep the choice at the project level via **Override options for this project only**, so you do not accidentally drag another client's settings into a new project.
